executive_summary.one_paragraph: SRC is canonically an intracellular N-myristoylated kinase, but in cancer cells it undergoes topological inversion onto the outer plasma membrane leaflet (eSrc) via autophagolysosomal exocytosis (ALE). eSrc is detected in primary human tumors and xenografts; anti-Src antibodies mediate tumor cell killing in vitro and in vivo. Surface accessibility is therefore high but strictly cancer-state-dependent — normal cells retain SRC intracellularly. Under normal conditions SRC shows junctional/vesicular localization with limited systemic reachability.

surface_evidence.grade_rationale: All direct surface-accessibility evidence originates from a single source (PMID:41818370) and its companion paper (PMID:41818382), both reporting the same discovery that Src undergoes topological inversion onto the cancer cell surface via autophagolysosomal exocytosis (ALE). The functional assays (antibody-mediated tumor killing in xenografts, eSrc detection in primary tumors) and the companion abstract corroborate surface exposure, but they rely on a single method class—live-cell antibody engagement/functional killing assays—and are not truly independent methodological approaches (e.g., no separate surface biotinylation or flow cytometry panel). A contradicting IHC observation (a1_evi_06) scores SRC as cytoplasmic in tumor tissue, but this is expected given that standard permeabilized IHC detects the predominant intracellular pool and does not negate the novel eSrc surface form. No meaningful contradiction undermines the surface claim; the IHC simply reflects the canonical localization. Grade: direct_single_method.

expression_observations.context: SRC cytoplasmic staining in tumor tissue sections scored on four-tiered system (negative/weak/moderate/strong); assessed as cytoplasmic not membranous

antibodies.name: anti-Src (therapeutic antibody targeting eSrc extracellular face)
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Used therapeutically to target extracellular-facing eSrc on cancer cell surface; validated by functional tumor cell killing in vitro and xenograft efficacy in vivo.

expression_observations.context: Src noncanonically translocated and topologically inverted onto cancer cell surface (eSrc) in vitro in cancer cell lines via autophagolysosomal exocytosis (ALE)
eSrc detected in primary human tumors; anti-Src antibody therapies targeting extracellular face mediated tumor cell killing in cell culture
eSrc detected in mouse xenograft tumor models; anti-Src antibody therapies effective in vivo
Exocytosis exposes Src at the outer surface of cancer cells, poised for therapeutic targeting (independent companion paper PMID:41818382)

non_surface_expression.context: Human tumor tissue sections (cancer, unspecified type); SRC scored for cytoplasmic staining intensity by IHC in a four-tiered system (negative/weak/moderate/strong), indicating predominant intracellular/cytoplasmic localization in standard pathological assessment.

therapeutic_engagement.description: Anti-Src antibody-based therapeutics targeting the extracellular face of eSrc (extracellular membrane-associated Src) have been demonstrated to mediate tumor cell killing in cell culture systems and in mouse xenograft models (PMID:41818370). eSrc was also detected in primary human tumors. The program is preclinical; no named sponsor or clinical trial is documented in this ledger. The therapeutic rationale is based on cancer-specific topological inversion of Src onto the outer plasma membrane surface, enabling antibody access to an otherwise intracellular kinase.
therapeutic_engagement.surface_form_rationale: Src is canonically an intracellular N-myristoylated protein. In cancer, it undergoes topological inversion via autophagolysosomal exocytosis (ALE), exposing it at the outer plasma membrane as eSrc. Anti-Src antibody therapeutics engage this cancer-specific surface-exposed form. Standard IHC scores SRC as cytoplasmic (a1_evi_06), underscoring that the surface form is a cancer-specific, non-secreted membrane-anchored topology rather than a constitutive or secreted isoform.

dual_localization.condition: high invadopodia activity GBM cancer cells

anatomical_accessibility.rationale: HPA flags SRC as a junctional protein localized to cell junctions and plasma membrane. Junction-restricted proteins are shielded by tight junctions from systemic/blood-side access, limiting reachability for systemically delivered binders.

anatomical_accessibility.context: cancer cells and primary tumors (extracellular Src, eSrc)
anatomical_accessibility.rationale: In cancer, SRC undergoes topological inversion onto the outer plasma membrane leaflet via autophagolysosomal exocytosis, exposing it extracellularly. Tumor vasculature is leaky, so systemically delivered antibodies can reach eSrc on cancer cells in vivo.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Normal (non-cancer) cells where SRC is an N-myristoylated intracellular kinase localized to the cytoplasmic face of the plasma membrane and intracellular compartments
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: Cancer cells (in vitro cell lines and in vivo primary human tumors) with active oncogenic transformation
accessibility_modulation.change: SRC undergoes non-canonical topological inversion and translocation onto the outer leaflet of the plasma membrane (extracellular Src, eSrc), placing its protein domains at the extracellular face. This event is absent in normal cells and cancer-specific.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: eSrc is fully accessible to extracellular binders (antibodies, biologics) in cancer cells and primary tumors. Anti-Src antibodies mediate tumor cell killing in vitro and in mouse xenograft models, validating eSrc as a therapeutically actionable surface target restricted to disease state.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Non-cancer cells lacking significant autophagolysosomal exocytosis (ALE) activity; SRC remains intracellular
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: Cancer cell lines with prominent autophagolysosomal exocytosis (ALE) activity
accessibility_modulation.change: ALE drives fusion of autophagolysosomes with the plasma membrane, delivering SRC to the outer cell surface with topological inversion. This is the mechanistic secretory pathway responsible for eSrc surface exposure in cancer.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Surface SRC accessibility is mechanistically gated by ALE activity. Inhibiting ALE could abolish eSrc surface exposure; conversely, ALE-active cancers present eSrc as an accessible extracellular epitope for antibody-based therapeutics.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Multiple human cell line types (HPA panel) under baseline culture conditions
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: Steady-state across cell lines; SRC distributes among plasma membrane, cell junctions, and vesicles simultaneously
accessibility_modulation.change: SRC is detected at cell junctions, the plasma membrane, and vesicular compartments concurrently, reflecting constitutive dual/multi-localization rather than exclusive surface residence.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Surface-accessible SRC is confined to cell-cell junction zones and limited membrane regions under normal conditions. Junctional localization may restrict access to binders unless cell contacts are disrupted; vesicular pool is not accessible to extracellular agents.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Resting osteoblast-like MC3T3-E1 cells without mechanical stimulation; RANKL largely in intracellular stores
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: MC3T3-E1 osteoblast-like cells subjected to fluid shear stress, activating c-Src (Tyr416 phosphorylation)
accessibility_modulation.change: Fluid shear stress activates c-Src kinase activity, which facilitates redistribution of RANKL from intracellular compartments toward the cell periphery and membrane fraction, increasing membrane-proximal surface protein abundance.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Mechanical stimulation activates SRC-dependent surface trafficking pathways in osteoblasts, increasing accessibility of cargo proteins (e.g., RANKL) at the plasma membrane. SRC itself acts as an upstream regulator rather than the primary surface-exposed target in this context.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Early-stage (day 1) chondrogenic micromass cultures derived from embryonic chicken limb bud cells, prior to histodifferentiation
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: Differentiating chondrogenic cells at days 3, 6, 10, and 15 of micromass culture representing progressive histodifferentiation stages
accessibility_modulation.change: Temporal surfaceome profiling identifies SRC in chondrocyte surfaceome cluster 2 with potential differentiation-stage-dependent changes in surface abundance across the 15-day chondrogenic program, though specific quantitative shifts for SRC are not resolved.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: SRC surface accessibility in chondrocytes may vary with differentiation stage during cartilage development. Evidence is weak/ambiguous but supports that developmental context gates SRC surface presence in this specialized somatic lineage.

accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: High-invadopodia-activity GBM cancer cell lines with active invasion program
accessibility_modulation.change: In high-invadopodia GBM cells, SRC is enriched in small extracellular vesicles (sEVs) secreted alongside CTTN, CFL1, ITGA3, ITGB3, indicating cancer-invasion-state-associated peripheral/vesicular SRC accumulation and secretion.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: In invasive GBM, SRC associates with the cell periphery and is packaged into sEVs, suggesting surface-proximal localization that may be accessible to extracellular agents in this disease/invasion state. sEV-associated SRC could also serve as a circulating biomarker.

structure.source: AlphaFold DB (STUB — fetchers not yet built)

co_receptor_requirements.rationale: Surface exposure of eSrc is mechanistically gated by autophagolysosomal exocytosis (ALE) activity. ALE is prominent in cancer cell lines but not in normal cells, making the ALE pathway modulatory for eSrc surface delivery. No obligate co-receptor for surface retention is identified.

restricted_subdomain.rationale: HPA data flag SRC as a junctional protein localized to cell junctions, plasma membrane, and vesicles under baseline conditions. Junctional restriction limits access by systemically delivered binders. In cancer, eSrc redistributes to the apical/peripheral membrane, relieving this restriction but only in the disease state.

ecd_size_assessment.rationale: SRC is a non-transmembrane, N-myristoylated cytoplasmic kinase with no canonical extracellular domain. The eSrc surface form arises by topological inversion; the exposed surface comprises domains originally facing the cytoplasm. Accessible epitope size for extracellular binders is therefore non-standard and likely small-to-minimal relative to conventional surface receptors.

epitope_masking.rationale: Because eSrc results from topological inversion of a cytoplasmic kinase, epitopes accessible extracellularly are those not normally exposed to solvent in the canonical orientation. Conformational constraints from membrane anchorage and the non-native topology may mask portions of the kinase domain. No direct glycan-masking or partner-occlusion data are present in the ledger.

evidence.claim: Src (proto-oncogene tyrosine kinase, P12931) is noncanonically translocated and topologically inverted onto the cell surface in cancer cells, demonstrated both in vitro (cancer cell lines) and in vivo (animal models). This represents a novel surface-accessible form of an otherwise intracellular N-myristoylated protein, directly evidencing plasma membrane surface expression of Src in cancer contexts.

spans.quote: In this work, we found that Src is noncanonically translocated and inverted onto the cell surface in cancer, both in vitro and in vivo.

evidence.claim: Src, prototypically an intracellular N-myristoylated protein, undergoes topological inversion onto the cell surface in cancer. The extracellular face of Src (eSrc) is antibody-targetable. This claim establishes the topology of the surface-exposed form: the normally cytoplasmic kinase domain and/or N-terminal myristoylation-anchored domain is inverted to face the extracellular space. The mechanism involves autophagolysosomal exocytosis (ALE), making eSrc a cancer-specific surface topology.

spans.quote: Thus, intracellular N-myristoylated proteins, prototypically Src, can be topologically inverted onto the cell surface in cancer and targeted with antibody therapeutics.

evidence.claim: Autophagolysosomal exocytosis (ALE) is identified as the secretory mechanism by which Src is translocated to the cell surface in cancer cell lines. This pathway is prominent in cancer and drives surface exposure of normally intracellular Src. This is relevant to the shed/secreted form risk: ALE could produce both membrane-anchored eSrc and potentially secreted/shed forms of Src.

spans.quote: We identified autophagolysosomal exocytosis (ALE) as a secretory mechanism prominent in cancer cell lines.

evidence.claim: Extracellular membrane-associated Src (eSrc) was detected in primary human tumors. Anti-Src antibody-based therapies targeting the extracellular face of eSrc mediated tumor cell killing in cell culture systems and in mouse xenograft models. This constitutes therapeutic engagement evidence: preclinical antibody program (unnamed sponsor, preclinical stage) targeting the extracellular surface-exposed form of Src (eSrc) in cancer, engaging the outer plasma membrane face. Efficacy demonstrated in xenograft models supports surface accessibility for antibody targeting.

spans.quote: Furthermore, this extracellular membrane-associated Src (eSrc) was found in primary tumors, and anti-Src antibody-based therapies mediated tumor cell killing in cell culture systems and in mouse xenograft models.

evidence.claim: Independent source (companion paper PMID:41818382) corroborates that exocytosis exposes Src at the outer surface of cancer cells. The abstract asserts Src is poised at the outer plasma membrane surface for therapeutic targeting, providing a second independent line of surface-expression evidence from a distinct publication. This is a direct surface-expression claim for Src at the cancer cell surface.

spans.quote: Exocytosis exposes Src at the outer surface of cancer cells, poised for therapeutic targeting.

evidence.claim: IHC scoring rubric from a cancer tissue study evaluated SRC staining as cytoplasmic (not membranous), while AXL and c-MET were scored for membranous staining. The four-tiered system (negative/weak/moderate/strong) assessed cytoplasmic staining intensity for SRC specifically. This methodological note indicates that in standard IHC practice applied to this tumor cohort, SRC was evaluated as cytoplasmic, not surface/membranous — relevant as a non-surface-method tissue observation qualifying the surface claim.

spans.quote: AXL, SRC, and c‐MET staining was scored in a four‐tiered scoring system, evaluating membranous staining intensity for AXL and c‐MET, and cytoplasmic staining intensity for SRC as negative (score = 0), weak (score = 1), moderate (score = 2), or strong (score = 3).

evidence.claim: HPA database curates SRC subcellular localization to cell junctions, plasma membrane, and vesicles, supporting presence at the cell surface with additional junctional and vesicular compartments.

spans.quote: HPA subcellular locations: Cell Junctions;Plasma membrane;Vesicles.

evidence.claim: HPA flags SRC as a junctional protein, indicating cell-junction-restricted surface localization with implications for anatomical accessibility at cell-cell contact zones.

spans.quote: HPA flags this gene as junctional.

evidence.claim: In cancer cells and in vivo tumors, Src undergoes non-canonical translocation and topological inversion onto the outer cell surface (extracellular Src, eSrc), a cancer-specific surface accessibility event not observed in normal cells.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: cancer cell lines and in vivo tumor models

spans.quote: In this work, we found that Src is noncanonically translocated and inverted onto the cell surface in cancer, both in vitro and in vivo.

evidence.claim: Autophagolysosomal exocytosis (ALE) is identified as the secretory mechanism driving Src surface translocation in cancer cell lines, representing a cancer-state-induced accessibility modulation pathway. Baseline: normal/non-cancer cells lack surface Src. Modulating state: cancer, with ALE activity. Change: Src exposed at outer plasma membrane leaflet.

spans.quote: We identified autophagolysosomal exocytosis (ALE) as a secretory mechanism prominent in cancer cell lines.

evidence.claim: Extracellular membrane-associated Src (eSrc) is present in primary human tumors in vivo, confirming cancer-tissue-specific surface exposure of Src protein. Anti-Src antibody therapies mediate tumor cell killing in cell culture and mouse xenograft models, validating eSrc as an accessible surface target.

spans.quote: Furthermore, this extracellular membrane-associated Src (eSrc) was found in primary tumors, and anti-Src antibody-based therapies mediated tumor cell killing in cell culture systems and in mouse xenograft models.

evidence.claim: SRC, prototypically an N-myristoylated intracellular protein, undergoes topological inversion onto the cell surface specifically in cancer, placing its protein domains at the extracellular face. This cancer-specific surface exposure is mechanistically distinct from canonical surface-targeted transmembrane proteins.

spans.quote: Thus, intracellular N-myristoylated proteins, prototypically Src, can be topologically inverted onto the cell surface in cancer and targeted with antibody therapeutics.

evidence.claim: Exocytosis drives exposure of Src at the outer surface of cancer cells, with Src found poised at the extracellular face for therapeutic targeting. This represents a disease-state-induced (cancer) accessibility modulation: Src transitions from intracellular cytoplasmic localization in normal cells to outer plasma membrane exposure in cancer cells.

spans.quote: Exocytosis exposes Src at the outer surface of cancer cells, poised for therapeutic targeting.

evidence.claim: In osteoblast-like MC3T3-E1 cells, fluid shear stress activates c-Src (Tyr416 phosphorylation) and promotes redistribution of RANKL toward the cell periphery with increased membrane-fraction RANKL. This identifies c-Src activation by mechanical stimulation as a modulator of surface protein accessibility in osteoblastic cells.

spans.quote: Fluid shear stress increased c-Src activation (Tyr416 phosphorylation) and promoted redistribution of RANKL toward the cell periphery, accompanied by an increase of RANKL in the membrane fraction.

evidence.claim: A mechanistic model in osteoblastic cells (MC3T3-E1) shows that shear stress activates c-Src to facilitate RANKL trafficking from intracellular stores to membrane-proximal regions, linking mechanical stimuli to surface protein modulation via c-Src kinase activity.

spans.quote: Together, these data support a model in which shear stress activates c-Src to facilitate RANKL localization from intracellular stores toward membrane-proximal regions, thereby providing a mechanistic link between mechanical cues and osteoblast-derived osteoclastogenic signaling.

evidence.claim: In NIS surface trafficking network analysis, SRC (proto-oncogene tyrosine kinase Src) was identified as one of four key regulatory proteins in the hsa04810 pathway controlling plasma membrane trafficking of NIS candidates in thyroid/cancer cell contexts. SRC forms a tight interaction subnetwork with RAC1, ARP2/3 complex, and EZRIN regulating PM-targeted protein surface retention.

spans.quote: Further analysis with STRING software revealed that the 29 top-scoring NIS-PM candidates formed a tight interaction subnetwork around cytoplasmic actin (ACTB) and four of the regulatory proteins on the hsa04810 pathway ( Figure 3 A): the small GTPase RAC1 (RAS-related C3 botulinum toxin substrate 1), the proto-oncogene tyrosine-protein kinase Src (SRC), the actin-nucleating ARP 2/3 complex subunit 4 (ARPC4), and the actin-binding adaptor protein ezrin (EZRIN).

evidence.claim: The SRC/RAC1/PAK1/PIP5K/EZRIN signaling pathway is established as a key regulator of actin-cytoskeleton anchoring and retention of NIS at the plasma membrane, with SRC acting upstream in this surface-retention pathway in thyroid epithelial cells.

spans.quote: Biochemical and functional analysis allowed us to establish the SRC/RAC1/PAK1/PIP5K/EZRIN pathway as a key regulator of NIS actin-cytoskeleton anchoring and retention at the PM [ 10 ].

evidence.claim: SRC kinase is identified in the chondrocyte surfaceome (Surfaceome cluster 2) with a role in chondrocyte phenotype control, providing mass-spectrometry-based evidence for SRC protein presence at or near the cell surface in chondrocytes during cartilage development.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: embryonic chicken limb bud-derived chondrogenic cells

spans.quote: including RECK (a membrane-anchored matrix metalloprotease inhibitor [ 45 ]), FGFR2 (fibroblast growth factor receptor 2; regulates chondrocyte proliferation/differentiation signaling [ 46 ]), multiple ephrin receptors (EPHA7, EPHB2, EPHB1, EPHB3 with potential roles in cartilage morphogenesis [ 47 ]), SRC kinase (chondrocyte phenotype control [ 48 ]), and GPC1 (glypical-1; a proteoglycan and cell surface co-receptor involved in growth factor pathway modulation [ 49 ]), highlighting surfaceome mechanisms in cartilage development and regeneration.

evidence.claim: Temporal surfaceome profiling at days 1, 3, 6, 10, and 15 of chondrogenic micromass culture demonstrates cell-state-dependent modulation of the surface proteome across histodifferentiation stages; SRC inclusion in this dataset indicates potential differentiation-stage-dependent surface accessibility changes.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: embryonic chicken limb bud-derived chondrogenic cells

spans.quote: Temporal proteomic profiling was conducted at days 1, 3, 6, 10, and 15 of micromass culture corresponding to key histodifferentiation stages [ 17 , 18 ], combining sialoglycoprotein enrichment via aminooxy-biotin conjugation with high-resolution LC-MS/MS on 3 biological replicates ( n = 3).

evidence.claim: In high-invadopodia-activity GBM cell lines, SRC is enriched in small extracellular vesicles (sEVs) alongside invadopodia regulators (CTTN, CFL1, ITGA3, ITGB3), suggesting that in invasive GBM cancer cells, SRC associates with the cell periphery and is secreted in vesicles — a disease/state-associated surface-proximal localization.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: GBM (glioblastoma) cell lines with high invadopodia activity

spans.quote: Furthermore, these high invadopodia activity cell lines secreted sEVs with a greater abundance of proteins involved in the regulation of invadopodia formation (CTTN, CFL1, SRC, ITGA3, ITGB3 – Cluster 2) and proteolytic activity (MMP2, MMP14, BSG/CD147 – Cluster 2) (Fig. 3 D).

confidence_reasoning: A1 grades evidence as direct_single_method: surface accessibility data derive from one primary source cluster (PMID:41818370/41818382) using functional antibody-engagement assays — a single method class without independent surface biotinylation or flow cytometry. No contradicting evidence undermines the core claim, but the evidence base is narrow. A2 adds strong state-dependence: eSrc surface exposure is strictly cancer-dependent via ALE; normal cells retain SRC intracellularly. This high state-dependence lowers therapeutic confidence (target is absent on normal cells but also absent on non-ALE-active tumors). Taken together: moderate confidence — compelling mechanistic claim from recent publications, but single-method, single-source, and highly state-gated.
